pipeline.area: Schema catalog vs real NetSuite — transactionline semantics
pipeline.issue: The catalog and demo data model transactionline as clean, positive item rows. Real NetSuite transactionline includes the main line, tax lines, shipping and COGS lines (mainline/taxline/accountinglinetype flags — all absent from the catalog), and on sales transactions the income lines carry NEGATIVE netamount (and typically negative quantity). The prompt never states any of this, and two examples teach SUM(l.netamount) AS revenue with no line-type filter.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:26-39 (transactionline has only id/transaction/item/quantity/rate/netamount/dims — no mainline, taxline, accountinglinetype; note just says 'in transaction currency'); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.js:281-293 (seeds only positive item lines, no header/tax rows); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:65-72 and 143-149 (few-shot 'best-selling items' and 'revenue by department' do SUM(l.netamount)/SUM(l.quantity) with no mainline='F'/taxline='F' filter); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:5-14 (no sign-convention or line-filter rule).
pipeline.impact: Copied to a live account, these queries return negative or near-zero 'revenue' (income lines negative + main line netting), double-count via tax/shipping rows, and ORDER BY units DESC inverts best-sellers. Numbers look plausible in the demo and dead wrong in production — the classic silent failure.
pipeline.fix_direction: Add mainline/taxline/accountinglinetype columns and explicit sign-convention notes to the catalog; add a hard prompt rule ('on sales transactions item netamount is negative; always filter mainline="F" AND taxline="F"'); seed the demo with signed data + main/tax rows so the demo actually exercises the real convention; update the two few-shot examples.

pipeline.area: Schema catalog vs real NetSuite — transaction.status labels
pipeline.issue: The catalog teaches status as human labels ('open' | 'paid' | 'pendingFulfillment' | 'pendingReceipt' | 'closed') and examples filter on them. Real SuiteQL transaction.status returns internal per-type codes (e.g. 'A'/'B'), with labels only via BUILTIN.DF(status) — which the prompt explicitly bans.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:14 (status note lists label strings); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.js:136-144 (STATUSES seeds those labels); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:48,116,137 (WHERE t.status = 'open' / 'pendingFulfillment'); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:11 ('Avoid BUILTIN.*' rule removes the one legitimate escape hatch).
pipeline.impact: Every status-filtered query ('open invoices', 'orders pending fulfillment', 'unpaid bills') returns 0 rows in a real account — the user reads 'no open invoices' as a true answer. This is the highest-traffic query class for AR/AP users.
pipeline.fix_direction: Document real status code semantics per type in the catalog (or add a status-mapping note), scope the BUILTIN ban to the demo translator only (teach BUILTIN.DF for display, code filters for WHERE), and seed the demo with real codes so demo behaviour matches production.

pipeline.area: Schema catalog vs real NetSuite — currency/consolidation semantics
pipeline.issue: The catalog states exchangerate converts 'to AUD base' and transactionaccountingline debit/credit are 'AUD base'. In real NetSuite, exchangerate converts to the transaction's OWN subsidiary base currency and TAL debit/credit are in that per-subsidiary base. The flagship consolidation few-shot (foreigntotal * exchangerate AS revenue_aud) and the P&L few-shot (sums TAL across all subsidiaries with no filter) bake this wrong model into generated SQL.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:17 ('to AUD base; demo uses static rates'), 44-49 ('GL impact rows... in AUD base currency'); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.js:77 (FX_TO_AUD static map applied to every sub), 174-208 (glFor converts everything to AUD); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:20-30 (P&L sums debit/credit across subsidiaries), 165-173 (ROUND(SUM(t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate),2) AS revenue_aud).
pipeline.impact: In a real multi-subsidiary account these queries silently add USD + GBP + AUD amounts and label the total as one currency. P&L and 'revenue in AUD' outputs look completely plausible while being financially meaningless — exactly the wrong-looking-right class.
pipeline.fix_direction: Correct the catalog notes (exchangerate → subsidiary base), add a prompt rule requiring a subsidiary filter or consolidatedexchangerate for cross-subsidiary money math, and replace the consolidation few-shot with a per-subsidiary version plus an explicit caveat.

pipeline.area: Schema catalog vs real NetSuite — string enums where production has FKs
pipeline.issue: customer.territory ('APAC'|'EMEA'|'AMER'), vendor.category ('Inventory'|...), and currency.name ('AUD'|'USD'|...) are modeled as filterable strings. In real NetSuite, territory and vendor category are numeric FKs (territory / vendorcategory tables), and the ISO code lives in currency.symbol while currency.name is the display name — the demo has these reversed (name='AUD', symbol='A$').
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:87 (territory string enum), 100 (vendor.category string enum), 184-188 (currency.name "'AUD' | 'USD' | 'GBP' | 'EUR'"); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.js:70-75 (name:'AUD', symbol:'A$'); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:77-82 (GROUP BY c.territory served as a curated answer).
pipeline.impact: Copied queries like WHERE c.territory='APAC' or cur.name='AUD' return 0 rows in production (silently 'no APAC revenue'), and GROUP BY c.territory / v.category emits raw internal ids as segment labels. Users trust segment breakdowns that never materialize or mislabel.
pipeline.fix_direction: Model these as FKs with their lookup tables in the catalog (or annotate 'numeric FK in real accounts — join territory/vendorcategory'), fix the currency name/symbol semantics in both catalog and demoData, and update the affected examples.

pipeline.issue: The keyword matcher scores on 5-character prefixes, accepts a single-word overlap as a match, breaks ties by array order, and — worst — returns EXAMPLES[0] ('Top 10 customers by revenue') for ANY question with zero keyword overlap. The response carries an empty explanation and only a subtle 'Curated' badge, so nothing tells the user the SQL answers a different question.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:183 (w.startsWith(k.slice(0,5)) scoring), 189 (return bestScore > 0 ? best : EXAMPLES[0]); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:103-108 (explanation: ''); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:261 (badge is the only signal). Concrete misfires: 'top items by revenue' scores top-customers (top+revenue=2) over best-selling-items (items=1) and returns customer SQL; 'New York customers' prefix-matches 'new'→'Customers created in last 12 months'; 'GL balance by account' matches nothing → top-10-customers SQL.
pipeline.impact: In keyless mode (the documented default — README says the console 'still works'), users receive confidently rendered SQL that answers a different question, with real results in the grid. Copied to NetSuite it runs fine and reports the wrong thing.
pipeline.fix_direction: Require a minimum score (≥2 or normalized threshold), return the matched example's question text as the explanation ('closest curated example: …'), and return an honest 'no curated match — add an API key' error instead of defaulting to EXAMPLES[0].

pipeline.area: API route — zero server-side validation, no repair loop
pipeline.issue: The route returns whatever the model emits, verbatim: no check that it is a single SELECT, no identifier validation against the TABLES catalog it already has in memory, no dry-run/EXPLAIN against the seeded SQLite DB (which runs fine in Node — the test file proves it), and when client-side execution fails the error is displayed and dropped — never fed back for a corrective second pass.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:54-56 (JSON.parse then return, Anthropic path) and 89-94 (Gemini path, only truthiness check on sql); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:157-160 (run() catch → setError only); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.test.mjs:57-63 (proves server-side seeded execution is trivially available); read-only enforcement exists only at client execution time (/Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/sqlite.js:23-25), so non-SELECT or hallucinated-column SQL is still displayed and copyable.
pipeline.impact: Hallucinated tables/columns (e.g. t.duedate for an aging question — not in the schema) reach the user unchecked; a whole class of fixable one-shot errors ships as-is. Prompt-injected DML would be shown with a Copy button even though the demo refuses to run it.
pipeline.fix_direction: Server-side: enforce single-statement SELECT, lint identifiers against TABLES, dry-run against a seeded sql.js instance, and on failure do one repair round-trip appending the SQL error to the conversation before returning.

pipeline.area: API route — fallback chain gated on key presence, not failure
pipeline.issue: If ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set, any Anthropic error (overload, invalid model id, truncated/unparseable JSON, network) returns 429/500 directly — it never falls through to Gemini or the curated examples. Only the Gemini block falls through on failure (its own comment says so), making the advertised 3-tier fallback unreachable in the primary path.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:57-63 (catch returns Response.json 429/500 inside the Anthropic branch) vs 67-68 ('Any failure (incl. free-tier 429) falls through to the curated examples below') and 98-100 (Gemini catch falls through). JSON.parse at route.js:55 also throws into this catch on stop_reason='max_tokens' truncation.
pipeline.impact: Users with a configured key see hard 'Generation failed' errors during any provider hiccup even though two working fallbacks sit directly below in the same function.
pipeline.fix_direction: Restructure so the Anthropic branch falls through to Gemini/examples on any non-refusal failure (optionally still surfacing a degraded-mode notice), and handle stop_reason 'max_tokens' explicitly.

pipeline.area: Dialect bridge — demo green-lights SQL that real SuiteQL rejects
pipeline.issue: The translator is one-directional (SuiteQL→SQLite) and never flags SQLite-only constructs, so the permissive demo engine validates SQL that fails in production: LIMIT (prompt asks for FETCH FIRST but nothing detects violations, and Gemini adherence is unverified), WITH/CTEs and window functions (unsupported in SuiteQL, fine in SQLite), GROUP BY alias or ordinal (Oracle rejects, SQLite accepts), bare ISO string date comparisons (trandate is TEXT in the demo — seed works, real SuiteQL needs TO_DATE), and case-insensitive LIKE (SQLite matches '%harbor%', Oracle-flavoured SuiteQL returns 0 rows). The editor's highlighter even legitimizes LIMIT/IFNULL/STRFTIME/DATE as keywords.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/translate.js:13-35 (rewrites only, no lint of SQLite-isms); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/seed.js:17 (trandate TEXT makes '2026-01-01' string compares work); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:10-11 (rules mention FETCH FIRST/NVL but never ban CTE/window/GROUP-BY-alias/LIMIT-output or state case sensitivity); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:47-49 (KEYWORDS includes LIMIT|IFNULL|STRFTIME|DATE).
pipeline.impact: A query that runs green in the demo — the product's core trust signal — errors in a real account ('Invalid or unsupported search' for CTE/window/LIMIT/GROUP BY alias) or silently returns 0 rows (case-mismatched LIKE from a lowercase user question). Demo success is systematically false assurance.
pipeline.fix_direction: Add a pre-display SuiteQL lint (reject/flag LIMIT, WITH, OVER(), GROUP BY alias/ordinal, unquoted date-string comparisons; suggest UPPER() for LIKE), add matching negative rules to the prompt, and remove SQLite tokens from the highlighter.

pipeline.area: Dialect bridge — valid SuiteQL fails or silently empties in the demo
pipeline.issue: The reverse direction is equally leaky: only 4 TO_CHAR masks are mapped; the TO_CHAR/ADD_MONTHS regexes use [^,]+ so any nested call (TO_CHAR(ADD_MONTHS(SYSDATE,-1),'YYYY-MM')) is left untranslated because ADD_MONTHS is rewritten AFTER TO_CHAR; TO_DATE discards its format mask, so non-ISO masks become date('01/31/2026') → NULL → WHERE silently matches nothing; EXTRACT/TRUNC/DECODE/CONCAT/NVL2 have no mapping; and the transaction-quoting rewrite also mangles string literals containing the word 'transaction'.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/translate.js:6-11 (four masks only), 23-25 ([^,]+ arg capture; TO_CHAR pass runs before ADD_MONTHS at line 29), 27 (TO_DATE(...) → date($1), mask dropped — silent NULL for 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 32 (\btransaction\b replacement applies inside quoted literals).
pipeline.impact: Correct, production-valid SuiteQL either errors in the demo ('no such function: TO_CHAR') or silently returns 0 rows (dropped TO_DATE mask), training users to 'fix' correct queries into demo-only dialect before copying them to NetSuite. Also breaks AI output whenever the model nests date functions — a very common pattern.
pipeline.fix_direction: Extend function mappings, honor TO_DATE masks (or reject unknown ones loudly), use a balanced-paren argument matcher or a real tokenizer, skip string literals during rewrites, and surface 'untranslatable construct' as an explicit message instead of a raw SQLite error.

pipeline.area: Prompt construction — missing rules and forced answers
pipeline.issue: The system prompt omits: the current date (relative questions like 'this quarter' are guesswork), a refusal/insufficient-schema path — the output schema REQUIRES a sql string for every input, so 'what's the weather' or 'age my receivables by due date' must produce SQL — any negative examples, any sign-convention/mainline/case-sensitivity/consolidation rules (see criticals above), and any guidance to use accountingperiod/postingperiod for financial reporting (few-shots group by TO_CHAR(trandate), which won't tie to NetSuite period-based reports). It also bans all BUILTIN.* — the standard real-account mechanism for label display.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:5-14 (full rule list — six bullets), 23-31 (OUTPUT_SCHEMA required:['sql','explanation'], no error/notes field), 11 ('Avoid BUILTIN.*'); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js:20-30,36-40 (date grouping exclusively via TO_CHAR(trandate)).
pipeline.impact: Unanswerable questions yield confident hallucinated SQL (invented columns error visibly; approximations — trandate-based 'aging' — pass silently); month-grouped financials won't reconcile with NetSuite's own period reports; real-account idioms are actively suppressed.
pipeline.fix_direction: Inject today's date and the demo data range; add an optional 'error'/'caveats' field to the output schema with an explicit refusal rule; add the negative rules enumerated in the critical findings; scope the BUILTIN ban to demo execution rather than generation.

pipeline.area: Few-shot coverage vs the question space
pipeline.issue: Only 7 of 15 examples are sent as few-shots, and all are single-aggregate GROUP BYs. Zero few-shots demonstrate: any WHERE date filter (ADD_MONTHS appears only in a non-fewshot example), status filtering, credit/payment netting (AR balance = invoices − credits − payments), accountingperiod joins, HAVING, or subqueries. The schema itself lacks duedate and amountremaining/foreignamountunpaid, so AR aging and DSO — top real-world asks — are inexpressible.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:19 (EXAMPLES.filter(e => e.fewshot)); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/examples.js (fewshot:true only on lines 7,19,35,64,122,154,164; ADD_MONTHS only at 87-90 which is not fewshot); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:5-24 (transaction has no duedate, no amountremaining).

Both are presented as authoritative.
pipeline.fix_direction: Add duedate + foreignamountunpaid to catalog and demo data; broaden few-shots to cover date ranges, netting across types, period joins, and one 'not answerable' exemplar; send all curated examples (15 is cheap) or select few-shots by embedding similarity to the question.

pipeline.issue: Execution failures are rendered and discarded: no client→server error report, no server-side logging of question→SQL→outcome (only console.error on provider exceptions), no eval set accumulation. Worse, remember() persists the generated SQL to localStorage BEFORE run() executes it, so known-broken queries are saved and replayed verbatim from the Recent sidebar; user edits that fix a query are never re-saved.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:141-142 (remember(q, data.sql) then await run(data.sql)), 157-160 (catch → setError only), 324-339 (history replays stored SQL untouched); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:61,96 (console.error is the only telemetry, and only for provider errors — successful-but-wrong generations leave zero trace).
pipeline.impact: The pipeline cannot learn: nobody can see which questions produce failing or wrong SQL, the fallback matcher's misfires are invisible, and users re-trigger past failures from history with a visible error each time.
pipeline.fix_direction: Record outcome (ok/error + message) per generation, persist it with history, POST failures to a lightweight /api/feedback log, and use that corpus to drive the repair loop and example curation. Move remember() after a successful run or store the status flag.

pipeline.area: Read-only guard — blacklist false positives, execution-only enforcement
pipeline.issue: isReadOnly is a word blacklist tested against the whole string, so legitimate literals trip it (WHERE memo LIKE '%update%' or memo = 'create' → 'Only SELECT queries are allowed'). Conversely it only runs at execution time in the browser — the server returns and the UI copies non-SELECT text freely.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/translate.js:37-39 (regex blacklist incl. CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE with \b boundaries — matches inside quoted literals); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/sqlite.js:23-25 (only enforcement point); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:263-266 (Copy available regardless).
pipeline.impact: Valid memo/text-search queries fail visibly in the demo; and the guard provides no protection for the copy-to-production path (ties into the missing server-side validation).
pipeline.fix_direction: Replace with a positive check (statement must start with SELECT, single statement, after stripping literals/comments) enforced server-side and client-side.

pipeline.area: UI trust cues around generated SQL
pipeline.issue: The provenance and health of the SQL is under-communicated: curated-fallback answers arrive with an empty explanation and only a small 'Curated' badge (nothing shows WHICH canned question was matched); Copy is enabled even when the query was never run or just failed; the AI badge is identical for Claude and Gemini; and the bar chart silently ignores middle columns of 3-column results.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/api/generate/route.js:104-108 (explanation: '' on fallback, matched example's question not returned); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/app/page.js:260-266 (badges + ungated Copy), 398-423 (BarChart uses row[0] and last column only).
pipeline.impact: Users cannot distinguish 'the model answered my question' from 'nearest canned example' or 'this SQL failed to execute' at the moment they hit Copy — amplifying every upstream wrongness finding.
pipeline.fix_direction: Surface the matched example question as the fallback explanation, disable or warn on Copy when the last run failed, badge the actual engine, and label omitted chart columns.

pipeline.issue: README and the schema header assert 'the SQL you see is genuine SuiteQL that transfers to a live account' and 'field names follow the real NetSuite analytics/SuiteQL tables so generated queries transfer', while the criticals above (status labels, netamount signs, FK-vs-string enums, AUD-base fiction, SQLite permissiveness) make transfer unreliable in exactly the high-traffic cases. Also the test file comment says '17 tables' while asserting 15 — the validation suite only proves examples run in the demo, never against real SuiteQL.
pipeline.evidence: /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/README.md:41-43 (transfer claim) and 45 (read-only 'rejected client-side' presented as the safety story); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/schema.js:1-2 (same claim); /Users/Bivek.Shah/Documents/fable/netsuite/suitesense/lib/demoData.test.mjs:14-15 (comment/assert mismatch; demo-only validation).
pipeline.impact: Sets the exact expectation — demo success equals production correctness — that the pipeline cannot honor, increasing the chance users paste unverified SQL into live accounts.
pipeline.fix_direction: Add a 'known divergences from live NetSuite' section, a verify-in-sandbox caveat next to the Copy button, and (per the roadmap's RESTlet live mode) a real-account smoke test for the curated examples.

divergences.demo_behavior: schema.js declares status as plain words: 'open' | 'paid' | 'pendingFulfillment' | 'pendingReceipt' | 'closed'; demoData.js STATUSES seeds those literals and examples.js filters t.status = 'open' / 'pendingFulfillment'.
divergences.real_behavior: Real SuiteQL transaction.status returns type-prefixed internal codes: 'CustInvc:A' (Open), 'CustInvc:B' (Paid In Full), 'SalesOrd:B' (Pending Fulfillment), 'SalesOrd:G' (Billed), 'VendBill:A' (Open), etc. Human-readable text ('Paid In Full') only comes via BUILTIN.DF(transaction.status). There is no 'paid' or 'open' literal.
divergences.breaks_what: Every status-filtered query: 'open invoices', 'paid invoices', 'orders pending fulfillment', 'open vendor bills'. WHERE t.status = 'open' returns ZERO rows on a real account — silent empty result, no error. This is the single most common AR/AP question class.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Change demoData STATUSES to real codes ('CustInvc:A', 'CustInvc:B', 'SalesOrd:B', 'SalesOrd:G', 'PurchOrd:B', 'VendBill:A', 'VendBill:B', ...) and update the schema.js note to enumerate them per type; add a prompt rule: 'status is a type-prefixed code; for display text use BUILTIN.DF(t.status); never compare against English words'. Update examples.js filters.

divergences.aspect: transactionline population: mainline, tax, COGS, shipping rows
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo transactionline contains exactly one row per sellable item line and nothing else — no mainline column, no header row, no tax/COGS/shipping/discount lines. Examples do naive SUM(l.netamount) grouped by item/department with no line-type filter.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transactionline includes a mainline header row (mainline = 'T'), tax lines (taxline = 'T'), COGS lines, shipping/discount lines, and GL-impact rows. The bundled finance query-patterns reference computes transaction totals as SUM(ABS(tl.amount)) / 2 precisely because the line table carries both sides of the posting. Item analytics require mainline = 'F' AND taxline = 'F' (and COGS exclusion) or amounts double count.
divergences.breaks_what: All line-grain aggregates: 'best-selling items', 'revenue by department/class', SKU mix, quantity sold. On a real account SUM(netamount) without mainline/taxline filters roughly doubles revenue and mixes GST and COGS into 'sales'. Silent wrong numbers, not errors.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Add mainline ('T'/'F'), taxline, and a header row + a tax line per invoice/bill to demoData so unfiltered SUMs are visibly wrong in the demo too; add prompt rule: 'line-level amount queries MUST filter mainline = 'F' AND taxline = 'F''. Update examples accordingly.

divergences.demo_behavior: demoData writes netamount as a positive number for invoice item lines (quantity * rate * jitter) and foreigntotal positive (negative only for CustCred). Examples treat SUM(netamount) as positive revenue and ORDER BY revenue DESC.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transactionline.netamount / amount follow GL sign convention: income (credit) lines on an invoice are NEGATIVE; the header foreigntotal is positive. Vendor bill expense lines are positive (debit). The bundled query-patterns' SUM(ABS(tl.amount))/2 idiom exists because signs differ across lines of one transaction.
divergences.breaks_what: Any line-level revenue metric: totals come out negative, ORDER BY ... DESC ranks the SMALLEST customers/items first, margin calcs (revenue minus COGS) get signs inverted, and CASE-based P&L from transactionline flips. Numbers are silently wrong in sign and ranking.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Flip demo netamount to credit-negative for CustInvc income lines (positive for VendBill), OR keep demo simple but add a hard prompt rule: 'on a real account use -netamount (or SUM(-tl.netamount)) for income lines, or aggregate from transactionaccountingline credit-debit'. Changing the demo data is safer since few-shot examples dominate generation.

divergences.demo_behavior: schema.js and demoData ACCOUNTS use accttype 'COGS'; the fewshot P&L example filters a.accttype IN ('COGS', 'Expense').
divergences.real_behavior: Real SuiteQL account.accttype for cost of goods sold is 'CostOfGoodsSold' (confirmed verbatim in the bundled netsuite-finance-analyst query-patterns.md: accttype IN ('Income', 'Expense', 'CostOfGoodsSold')).
divergences.breaks_what: P&L / margin / expense-breakdown queries: the COGS bucket silently matches zero accounts, so 'expenses' exclude all COGS and net income is overstated — the worst kind of silent financial error, and it is baked into a fewshot example.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Rename the demo value to 'CostOfGoodsSold' in schema.js note, demoData ACCOUNTS, and the P&L example. Trivial fix, no prompt rule needed once data matches.

divergences.demo_behavior: Demo defines exchangerate as a static rate 'to AUD base' regardless of subsidiary, and the fewshot 'revenue by subsidiary in AUD' example computes SUM(foreigntotal * exchangerate) as group-currency revenue.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transaction.exchangerate converts transaction currency to the OWNING SUBSIDIARY's base currency (a USD invoice on the US sub has rate 1.0, not 1.52-to-AUD). Group/consolidated reporting uses the consolidatedexchangerate table (period average/current/historical by account type), and the bundled docs state flatly that SuiteQL cannot replicate the consolidation engine — a cross-subsidiary SUM is a pre-elimination, pre-translation aggregate.
divergences.breaks_what: The consolidation fewshot and any 'total revenue across subsidiaries in AUD' query: on a real account foreigntotal * exchangerate yields each sub's LOCAL base amount, so USD and GBP revenue are summed as if they were AUD — silently wrong by the FX factor.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Fix the demo: make exchangerate subsidiary-relative (1.0 for same-currency txns) and either add a consolidatedexchangerate table or drop the AUD-consolidation fewshot; add prompt rule: 'exchangerate is to the subsidiary base currency; for group reporting join consolidatedexchangerate or defer to standard reports and label SuiteQL sums pre-elimination'.

divergences.aspect: transaction missing posting / approvalstatus / recordtype / void
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo transaction has no posting, approvalstatus, recordtype, void, createdby, or duedate columns; the only posting flag lives on transactionaccountingline, and schema notes say orders 'never post and have no rows here'.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transaction carries posting ('T'/'F'), approvalstatus (2 = approved), void, recordtype (lowercase 'custinvc' — distinct from type 'CustInvc'), createdby, duedate. The Oracle connector skill mandates posting = 'T' and approvalstatus = 2 on every GL-accurate query, and its recordtype table + patterns filter t.recordtype = 'custinvc'.
divergences.breaks_what: Two directions: (a) demo-trained queries omit posting/approval filters, so on a real account revenue includes unapproved, unposted, and voided transactions — inflated numbers; (b) queries written per Oracle guidance (t.recordtype = 'custinvc', t.posting = 'T') error in the demo because the columns don't exist, so the demo can't be used to rehearse correct real-world SQL. Also no duedate means AR aging cannot be expressed at all.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Add posting, approvalstatus, recordtype (lowercase), void, duedate to the demo schema/data; add prompt rule requiring posting = 'T' (and approvalstatus = 2 where relevant) on financial aggregates.

divergences.aspect: transactionaccountingline shape: no transactionline key, no amount/netamount, no accountingbook
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo TAL has only (transaction, account, debit, credit, posting) — one synthetic row set per transaction, joined on transaction alone; posting exists only here and only 'T' rows are generated.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transactionaccountingline is keyed by (transaction, transactionline) — the canonical join is ON tal.transaction = t.id AND tal.transactionline = tl.id — and additionally carries amount, netamount, posting, and accountingbook (multi-book accounts have one row set per book). Nonposting transactions can have rows with posting = 'F'. The bundled reference also warns TAL availability varies by endpoint/feature set.
divergences.breaks_what: Queries needing line dimensions with GL accounts (revenue by account AND department/class) can't be learned from the demo; on real multi-book accounts a demo-style join without accountingbook = primary double/triple counts every amount; relying on 'orders have no TAL rows' instead of posting = 'T' includes nonposting rows on real.
divergences.fix_direction: Change demo: add transactionline (link to the line id), amount/netamount, accountingbook (constant 1) columns; add prompt rule: 'join TAL on both transaction and transactionline; always filter tal.posting = 'T' and primary accounting book'.

divergences.aspect: accountingperiod missing isquarter / isyear / isadjust rollup rows
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo generates only 25 monthly rows; schema exposes id, periodname, startdate, enddate, closed. periodname like 'Jul 2025' is unique.
divergences.real_behavior: Real accountingperiod contains month rows PLUS quarter rollups ('Q1 2025'), year rollups ('FY 2025'), and adjustment periods, flagged by isquarter, isyear, isadjust. The bundled query-patterns period pattern filters isquarter = 'F' AND isyear = 'F' as standard practice; date-range joins on startdate/enddate without those filters match overlapping quarter/year rows.
divergences.breaks_what: Any query joining accountingperiod by date range or aggregating 'per period' triples/quadruples amounts on a real account (month + its quarter + its year rows all match); periodname lookups can hit adjustment periods. Demo-trained queries omit the flags because the columns don't exist.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Add isquarter/isyear/isadjust columns and a few quarter/year rows to the demo so unfiltered joins visibly duplicate; add prompt rule: 'always filter isquarter = 'F' AND isyear = 'F' AND isadjust = 'F' on accountingperiod'.

divergences.aspect: FETCH FIRST / OFFSET pagination vs ROWNUM
divergences.demo_behavior: Fewshot examples use FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY, and translate.js converts FETCH/OFFSET to SQLite LIMIT, so the demo actively teaches FETCH syntax.
divergences.real_behavior: The Oracle NetSuite AI-connector skill hard-rules 'No OFFSET/FETCH — use ROWNUM pagination' and 'NEVER skip ROWNUM <= 1000' for its SuiteQL endpoint. (Some SuiteQL surfaces do accept FETCH FIRST, so this is endpoint-dependent, but the target MCP/connector endpoint documented here rejects it.)
divergences.breaks_what: Every 'top N' query — top customers, top vendors, best sellers — errors outright on the connector endpoint, and unbounded queries violate the ROWNUM <= 1000 guardrail.
divergences.fix_direction: Mostly prompt/examples: rewrite fewshots to ROWNUM-style (or emit both and let a real-mode translator choose); optionally have translate.js accept ROWNUM so real-shaped queries also run in the demo. Verify against the actual target endpoint before committing, since SuiteQL REST does accept FETCH FIRST.

divergences.aspect: Date literals and TO_DATE format masks
divergences.demo_behavior: trandate is stored as TEXT ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD' in SQLite; bare string comparisons (t.trandate >= '2026-01-01') work, translate.js collapses any TO_DATE mask to date(literal), and only 4 TO_CHAR formats are whitelisted.
divergences.real_behavior: Real trandate is a true DATE. Both bundled references mandate TO_DATE('<date>', 'MM/DD/YYYY') masks; bare ISO string comparisons depend on session NLS format and typically fail or mismatch. Real SuiteQL supports the full Oracle TO_CHAR/TRUNC/LAST_DAY family (demo silently passes unsupported formats through, so a query that works on real can break in demo and vice versa).
divergences.breaks_what: Date-window filters — the backbone of every 'this quarter/last month' question. A demo-validated bare-string date filter errors or returns wrong rows on real; a real-correct TO_DATE('01/01/2026','MM/DD/YYYY') gets mangled by the demo translator (date('01/01/2026') is not a valid SQLite date).
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Prompt rule: 'always wrap dates in TO_DATE with an explicit mask, never bare strings'; fix translate.js to honor the mask when converting (MM/DD/YYYY -> ISO) and to leave unknown TO_CHAR formats as visible errors instead of silent passthrough.

divergences.aspect: transactionline missing account / amount / foreignamount columns
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo lines expose item, quantity, rate, netamount only — no account, amount (base currency), or foreignamount.
divergences.real_behavior: Real transactionline carries account (GL account per line), amount (base currency) and foreignamount (transaction currency) alongside netamount; both bundled references drive their canonical actuals patterns off tl.account and tl.amount (e.g. account LEFT JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.account = a.id).
divergences.breaks_what: The standard real-world actuals-by-account pattern is unlearnable from the demo, and demo-trained queries use netamount (foreign currency) where base-currency amount is needed — cross-currency aggregates mix currencies silently. Real-correct queries referencing tl.account/tl.amount error in the demo.
divergences.fix_direction: Change demo: add account, amount ( = netamount * exchangerate, signed), foreignamount to transactionline; then examples can use the same idioms the Oracle docs use.

divergences.aspect: Header total (foreigntotal) as revenue, and tax absence
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo has no tax lines, so foreigntotal exactly equals the sum of item netamounts; multiple fewshots use SUM(t.foreigntotal) WHERE type = 'CustInvc' as 'revenue', including across territories/subsidiaries in mixed currencies.
divergences.real_behavior: Real invoice foreigntotal is tax-INCLUSIVE (GST/VAT) and is in transaction currency; revenue is the tax-exclusive income-line total (or the Income posting on TAL). Summing header totals across entities also mixes currencies unless converted.
divergences.breaks_what: 'Top customers by revenue', 'monthly revenue trend', 'revenue by territory' overstate revenue by the tax rate (10% GST in AU) and, when customers span subsidiaries, add USD+GBP+AUD as raw numbers. Silent overstatement, matches neither the Income Statement nor management expectations.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Add a tax line + tax-inclusive foreigntotal to demo data so header-vs-line divergence is realistic; add prompt rule: 'revenue = income lines (mainline='F', taxline='F') or TAL Income credits, not header totals; convert currency explicitly'.

divergences.demo_behavior: Demo customer.territory is a varchar with literal values 'APAC' | 'EMEA' | 'AMER'; a fewshot groups revenue by c.territory directly.
divergences.real_behavior: Real customer.territory is a numeric reference to the territory table (sales territory feature); grouping by it yields internal IDs, and the values are account-specific names, never guaranteed to be 'APAC'/'EMEA'/'AMER'. Region analysis on real accounts usually goes through subsidiary, billcountry, or a custom field instead.
divergences.breaks_what: 'Revenue by territory/region' returns raw IDs (or errors if compared to 'APAC'), and WHERE c.territory = 'APAC' matches nothing. Silent empty/cryptic results.
divergences.fix_direction: Change demo: model territory as an id joining a small territory table (or drop it and use subsidiary/country for region examples); prompt rule: 'territory is a record reference — join or BUILTIN.DF it, and verify the account actually uses territories'.

divergences.demo_behavior: Demo inventorybalance has one row per (item, location) with quantityonhand/quantityavailable, always present.
divergences.real_behavior: Real 'inventorybalance' exists only on accounts with bin/inventory-status features and is grained per bin/status (item, location, binnumber, status) — multiple rows per item-location. The universally available per-location quantity table is aggregateitemlocation (quantityonhand, quantityavailable), or itemlocationmap depending on account. The bundled docs' metadata-guard section stresses exactly this class of table-availability variance.
divergences.breaks_what: 'Stock on hand by location' either errors (table not exposed) or double counts (summing bin/status rows without grouping) on real accounts; the demo never teaches the aggregateitemlocation fallback.
divergences.fix_direction: Both. Prefer renaming/basing the demo table on aggregateitemlocation semantics, or keep both names; prompt rule: 'probe table availability (metadata call or FETCH FIRST 1 probe) and fall back aggregateitemlocation <-> inventorybalance'.

divergences.aspect: Entity polymorphism (customer/vendor over one entity id-space)
divergences.demo_behavior: Demo keeps customer ids 1-40 and vendor ids 101-115 in one non-overlapping id space and joins transaction.entity to customer.id or vendor.id — structurally close to real.
divergences.real_behavior: Real NetSuite has a single polymorphic entity table; customer/vendor/employee are specialized views sharing that id space, joined via their id exactly as the demo does (both bundled references use c.id = t.entity). Differences: real also exposes the entity base table and BUILTIN.DF(t.entity) for name resolution regardless of type, and entityid on real is the auto-numbered/name string ('104 Harbor Logistics'), not 'CUST-104'.
divergences.breaks_what: Mostly survives. Breaks: mixed AR+AP listings (payments to vendors and customers in one query) — demo teaches separate joins where real would use entity/BUILTIN.DF; and any filter on entityid LIKE 'CUST-%' patterns matches nothing on real.
divergences.fix_direction: Prompt rule is enough: 'for type-agnostic entity names use BUILTIN.DF(t.entity) or the entity table; entityid formats are account-specific'. Optionally note in schema.js that CUST-/VEND- prefixes are demo-only.

divergences.demo_behavior: Demo item has a scalar baseprice used to seed rates.
divergences.real_behavior: The Oracle connector skill does list baseprice among item essential fields, but on real accounts with Multiple Prices/Multi-Currency (most mid-market accounts) list prices live in the pricing table (item, pricelevel, currency, unitprice) and item.baseprice is unpopulated/not queryable; effective sell price is the line rate anyway.
divergences.breaks_what: Queries like 'items priced above X' or margin-vs-list comparisons return nulls/errors on pricing-feature accounts. Low frequency in analytics workloads.
divergences.fix_direction: Prompt rule: 'prefer transactionline.rate for realized price; list price requires the pricing table on multi-price accounts'. Demo change optional (add a pricing table only if such questions matter).

divergences.demo_behavior: Demo currency.name is the ISO code ('AUD') and symbol is the glyph ('A$'); a fewshot selects cur.name as the currency code.
divergences.real_behavior: Real currency.name is the display name (e.g. 'Australian Dollar' / whatever the admin typed) while symbol holds the ISO code ('AUD'); displaysymbol holds the glyph. Filtering name = 'AUD' typically matches nothing.
divergences.breaks_what: Currency-labeled outputs show long names instead of codes; WHERE cur.name = 'USD' filters silently return empty.
divergences.fix_direction: Change demo (name = 'Australian Dollar', symbol = 'AUD', add displaysymbol) and add a one-line prompt note: 'ISO code lives in currency.symbol'.

divergences.demo_behavior: translate.js maps NVL -> IFNULL, but demo data has no nulls in amount fields and examples never use NVL, so generation is not conditioned to null-guard.
divergences.real_behavior: Real accounts have null amounts/memos/entities everywhere; the Oracle checklist requires NVL() on all nullable amount/text fields and notes ISNULL/IFNULL are invalid on the real endpoint (Oracle SQL).
divergences.breaks_what: Aggregates with arithmetic on nullable fields (amount - NVL-less credit) return NULL rows on real; any IFNULL the model learned from SQLite-flavored feedback errors outright on real.
divergences.fix_direction: Prompt rule ('use NVL, never IFNULL/ISNULL; NVL-wrap nullable amounts') plus seed a few null memos/employees in demoData so unguarded queries misbehave visibly.

gotchas.topic: transaction/transactionline join — duplicate rows without mainline filter
gotchas.rule: All transactions live in one `transaction` table and header queries that join `transactionline` return one row per line. To replicate the saved-search 'Main Line = true' behavior and avoid duplicate/double-counted transactions, add `transactionline.mainline = 'T'` to the join or WHERE. Join key is `transactionline.transaction = transaction.id`.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT t.tranid, t.foreigntotal FROM transaction t JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd' -- returns one row per line item, inflating counts and sums
gotchas.example_right: SELECT t.tranid, t.foreigntotal FROM transaction t JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id AND tl.mainline = 'T' WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'

gotchas.topic: Header fields that only exist on the mainline transactionline row
gotchas.rule: Several header-level fields are NOT exposed on the `transaction` table in the SuiteQL analytics (netsuite2.com) schema — notably `location` (removed), `subsidiary` (not exposed), and `createdfrom`. They must be read from the transactionline mainline row (`mainline='T'`). Each transaction has exactly one mainline row; for sales orders/fulfillments the mainline row's `item` is NULL (only work orders/assembly builds carry the built item on the mainline).
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT t.tranid, t.subsidiary, t.location FROM transaction t -- fails: subsidiary/location are not columns of transaction in SuiteQL
gotchas.example_right: SELECT t.tranid, tl.subsidiary, tl.location, tl.createdfrom FROM transaction t JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id AND tl.mainline = 'T'

gotchas.topic: Transaction status raw codes are single letters, not saved-search 'Type:Letter' strings
gotchas.rule: In SuiteQL, `transaction.status` returns bare single-letter codes ('A','B','C',...,'V','X','Y') that are only meaningful in combination with `transaction.type`. The saved-search style prefixed value ('CustInvc:A', 'SalesOrd:B') does NOT match in SuiteQL. Always filter type + status letter together, or compare on BUILTIN.DF(status) for the human string ('Invoice : Open').
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT id FROM transaction WHERE status = 'CustInvc:A' -- matches nothing; SuiteQL stores just 'A'
gotchas.example_right: SELECT id FROM transaction WHERE type = 'CustInvc' AND status = 'A' -- Invoice : Open. Discover codes with: SELECT type, status, BUILTIN.DF(status) FROM transaction GROUP BY type, status, BUILTIN.DF(status)

gotchas.topic: BUILTIN.DF returns a formatted display string — never filter/join on it expecting raw IDs
gotchas.rule: BUILTIN.DF(field) 'returns the display value of a field from the target record type without joining it explicitly', but the output is display-formatted text — e.g. BUILTIN.DF(createdfrom) yields 'Work Order #WO110', not 'WO110', and BUILTIN.DF(status) yields 'Sales Order : Billed'. Use it only in SELECT for labels; filter on raw IDs/codes, and for portable display names prefer explicit joins (e.g. join entity/item/account tables and select their name fields).
gotchas.example_wrong: WHERE BUILTIN.DF(tl.createdfrom) = 'WO110' -- fails: actual value is 'Work Order #WO110'; also slow
gotchas.example_right: SELECT c.entityid, c.companyname FROM transaction t JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity -- portable join; or SELECT BUILTIN.DF(t.entity) purely for display

gotchas.topic: Sign convention: transactionline amounts/quantities are ledger-signed (sales side is negative)
gotchas.rule: transactionline stores GL-signed values, not UI values: on invoices, item fulfillments, and work orders, `quantity` (and revenue-side netamount/foreignamount on item lines) is NEGATIVE even though the UI shows it positive; item receipts and sales-order demand lines are positive. Multiply by -1 (or use ABS/SUM of credit-debit) when reporting sales figures from line tables.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT SUM(tl.netamount) AS revenue FROM transactionline tl JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc' AND tl.mainline = 'F' -- revenue comes out negative
gotchas.example_right: SELECT SUM(tl.netamount) * -1 AS revenue FROM transactionline tl JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc' AND tl.mainline = 'F' AND tl.taxline = 'F'
gotchas.source: https://www.netsuitediagnostics.com/posts/transactions-inventory/ ('The quantity is sometimes negative even if on the UI it shows it as positive')

gotchas.topic: Tax lines must be excluded or sign-flipped when summing transaction lines
gotchas.rule: Rows with `transactionline.taxline = 'T'` carry tax amounts whose foreignamount must be multiplied by -1 (or subtracted) when computing transaction totals from lines; also exclude them (`taxline='F'`) when summing net merchandise amounts. Note quantities are always in the base unit of measure (a '5 Case (50 lb)' line stores quantity 250).
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT SUM(tl.foreignamount) FROM transactionline tl ... -- silently mixes tax lines into the merchandise total with the wrong sign
gotchas.example_right: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN tl.taxline = 'T' THEN tl.foreignamount * -1 ELSE tl.foreignamount END) ... -- or filter AND tl.taxline = 'F' for net-of-tax

gotchas.topic: GL impact: use transactionaccountingline with posting='T' on BOTH tables; debit/credit only exist there
gotchas.rule: Debit/credit columns are NOT exposed on transactionline; GL impact lives in `transactionaccountingline` (columns: account, amount, debit, credit, posting, netamount). Filter `transaction.posting = 'T' AND transactionaccountingline.posting = 'T'` to include only lines that actually hit the GL. For income accounts, net revenue = SUM(COALESCE(credit,0) - COALESCE(debit,0)). Join back to lines on BOTH keys: tal.transaction = tl.transaction AND tal.transactionline = tl.id.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT tl.debit, tl.credit FROM transactionline tl -- no such columns; and summing TAL without posting='T' includes non-posting amounts
gotchas.example_right: SELECT SUM(COALESCE(tal.credit,0) - COALESCE(tal.debit,0)) FROM transaction t JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account WHERE t.posting = 'T' AND tal.posting = 'T' AND a.accttype = 'Income'

gotchas.topic: Date literals: always TO_DATE with an explicit format mask, never bare strings
gotchas.rule: Comparing date columns to bare string literals depends on the account's date-format preference (US '1/15/2025' vs ISO) and breaks across accounts. Use TO_DATE('...','YYYY-MM-DD') (and TO_DATE with time elements for datetimes). BETWEEN works on dates once both bounds are TO_DATE'd. SYSDATE returns server-based current datetime; CURRENT_DATE returns the user's timezone-based datetime — prefer one deliberately.
gotchas.example_wrong: WHERE t.trandate >= '2025-01-01' -- format-preference dependent; may error or silently mismatch
gotchas.example_right: WHERE t.trandate BETWEEN TO_DATE('2025-01-01','YYYY-MM-DD') AND TO_DATE('2025-01-31','YYYY-MM-DD')

gotchas.topic: Datetime fields display date-only by default; use TO_CHAR/TRUNC; Oracle date functions supported
gotchas.rule: SuiteQL returns only the date portion of timestamp fields by default — use TO_CHAR(field,'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') to see time. Supported Oracle date functions include TO_CHAR, TO_DATE, TRUNC(date), ADD_MONTHS, LAST_DAY, NEXT_DAY, SYSDATE arithmetic (SYSDATE+1 day, +10/1440 minutes). Format masks like 'YYYY-MM-DD', 'MM', 'MONTH', 'DAY', 'Q', 'WW' work.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT lastmodifieddate FROM transaction -- time component silently dropped in output
gotchas.example_right: SELECT TO_CHAR(lastmodifieddate,'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS') FROM transaction WHERE lastmodifieddate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 7

gotchas.topic: Pagination: TOP and FETCH FIRST are supported, but OFFSET is SILENTLY IGNORED in the query text
gotchas.rule: SuiteQL supports SELECT TOP n and FETCH FIRST/NEXT n ROWS ONLY, but the SQL OFFSET clause 'is simply ignored, and no error is thrown' — every page returns the same first rows. Paginate either via the API layer (REST ?limit=&offset= query params, or N/query runSuiteQLPaged) or via nested-subquery ROWNUM ranges (ROWNUM is assigned before ORDER BY, so sort in an inner subquery first, then filter ROWNUM outside).
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT id FROM transaction ORDER BY id OFFSET 1000 ROWS FETCH NEXT 1000 ROWS ONLY -- OFFSET ignored; page 2 equals page 1
gotchas.example_right: SELECT * FROM (SELECT ROWNUM rn, x.* FROM (SELECT id, tranid FROM transaction ORDER BY id) x) WHERE rn BETWEEN 1001 AND 2000 -- or use REST /query/v1/suiteql?limit=1000&offset=1000

gotchas.topic: Row/governance limits differ per execution context (1000 REST page, 5000 N/query, 100k total)
gotchas.rule: REST /query/v1/suiteql returns max 1000 rows per page (limit param) and requires the 'Prefer: transient' header; total results via REST are capped at 100,000 unless SuiteAnalytics Connect is enabled. N/query query.runSuiteQL / runSuiteQLPaged in SuiteScript returns up to 5000 rows per call/page. An AI generator should emit bounded queries (aggregate or FETCH FIRST) or plan pagination rather than assume unlimited results.
gotchas.example_wrong: Issuing SELECT * FROM transactionline via REST and assuming all rows arrive in one response (silently truncated at the page limit)
gotchas.example_right: POST /services/rest/query/v1/suiteql?limit=1000&offset=0 with header 'Prefer: transient', then follow hasMore/offset pages; or aggregate server-side with GROUP BY

gotchas.topic: Accounting periods: exclude quarter/year/adjustment rollup rows and join via postingperiod
gotchas.rule: The `accountingperiod` table contains monthly periods PLUS quarter and year summary rows and adjustment periods. For monthly reporting filter isquarter = 'F' AND isyear = 'F' (and usually isadjust = 'F', isposting = 'T'); otherwise sums are double/triple-counted. Join transactions with transaction.postingperiod = accountingperiod.id, and filter posted GL activity by postingperiod rather than trandate for financial reports.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT ap.periodname, SUM(...) FROM accountingperiod ap JOIN transaction t ON t.postingperiod = ap.id GROUP BY ap.periodname -- includes Q1/FY rollup periods, double counting
gotchas.example_right: ... JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod WHERE ap.isquarter = 'F' AND ap.isyear = 'F' AND ap.isadjust = 'F' AND ap.isposting = 'T'

gotchas.topic: Booleans are 'T'/'F' character strings, not TRUE/FALSE
gotchas.rule: Every NetSuite boolean/checkbox field (mainline, posting, isinactive, taxline, isadjust, ...) is a one-character string compared against 'T' or 'F'. SQL boolean literals or 1/0 do not work.
gotchas.example_wrong: WHERE isinactive = false  -- or WHERE mainline = 1: error / no match
gotchas.example_right: WHERE isinactive = 'F' AND mainline = 'T'

gotchas.topic: Case sensitivity: identifiers are case-insensitive (transaction needs no quoting), string values are case-sensitive
gotchas.rule: Table/column names are case-insensitive and `transaction` is used unquoted in Oracle's own SuiteQL examples — no quoting/aliasing is required despite it being reserved in other SQL dialects. But STRING comparisons are case-sensitive Oracle semantics: type codes must match exact casing ('CustInvc', 'SalesOrd', 'VendBill'), and 'T'/'F' flags are uppercase.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT id FROM transaction WHERE type = 'CUSTINVC' -- returns 0 rows; wrong case on the value
gotchas.example_right: SELECT id FROM Transaction WHERE Type = 'CustInvc' -- identifier casing free, literal casing exact

gotchas.topic: GROUP BY strictness: every non-aggregated expression in SELECT/HAVING/ORDER BY must be in GROUP BY
gotchas.rule: SuiteQL enforces Oracle ORA-00979-style grouping: any column not wrapped in an aggregate must appear in GROUP BY, including expressions like BUILTIN.DF(field) — group by the identical expression. Violations surface as 'Search error occurred: Invalid or unsupported search' rather than a helpful grouping error. Ordering by an aggregate alias (ORDER BY total DESC) is allowed.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT type, status, COUNT(*) FROM transaction GROUP BY type -- status not grouped: 'Invalid or unsupported search'
gotchas.example_right: SELECT type, status, COUNT(*) AS n FROM transaction GROUP BY type, status ORDER BY n DESC

gotchas.topic: Don't mix SQL-92 and Oracle syntax in one query; prefer Oracle syntax
gotchas.rule: Official docs: 'SuiteQL supports the syntax for both SQL-92 and Oracle SQL', but 'you can't use both syntaxes in the same query', and Oracle warns that SQL-92-converted queries risk 'critical performance issues, such as time outs that aren't operationally remediable... You should use Oracle SQL syntax when creating SuiteQL queries.' SELECT DISTINCT, TOP n, UNION, HAVING, and subqueries are documented as supported (older community posts claiming DISTINCT is unsupported are outdated).
gotchas.example_wrong: Mixing dialects, e.g. SQL-92 join style plus Oracle-specific constructs in the same statement, or relying on non-Oracle functions like DATEADD/GETDATE
gotchas.example_right: Pure Oracle-style: SELECT DISTINCT email FROM transaction; SELECT TOP 10 * FROM transaction; TO_DATE/ADD_MONTHS/NVL for expressions

gotchas.topic: Currency: transaction.exchangerate for transaction-date base conversion; consolidatedexchangerate/BUILTIN.CONSOLIDATE for OneWorld rollups
gotchas.rule: foreignamount/foreigntotal are in the transaction currency; multiply by the transaction's stored `exchangerate` (rate on the transaction date) for subsidiary-base amounts, or use BUILTIN.CURRENCY_CONVERT(field, targetCurrency, date). For consolidated OneWorld reporting these are NOT enough: period-specific rates live in `consolidatedexchangerate` (postingperiod, fromsubsidiary, tosubsidiary, currentrate/averagerate/historicalrate) and BUILTIN.CONSOLIDATE(amount, 'LEDGER'|'INCOME', rateType, ...) applies them; income statement amounts use average rate, balance sheet current, equity historical.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT SUM(foreigntotal) FROM transaction -- sums mixed currencies; or using transaction exchangerate for consolidated P&L across subsidiaries
gotchas.example_right: SELECT trandate, foreigntotal, foreigntotal * exchangerate AS basetotal, BUILTIN.CURRENCY_CONVERT(foreigntotal, 2, trandate) AS basetotal2 FROM transaction WHERE id = 123; for consolidation: BUILTIN.CONSOLIDATE(tl.netamount, 'INCOME', 'DEFAULT', 'DEFAULT', <toSubsidiary>, t.postingperiod, ...)

gotchas.topic: Error 'Search error occurred: Invalid or unsupported search' — four distinct triggers
gotchas.rule: This generic error is triggered by: (1) referencing a field not exposed to SuiteQL, (2) query timeout (~5 minutes, e.g. under query.runPaged), (3) GROUP BY violations (non-aggregated, ungrouped column), (4) LISTAGG output exceeding roughly 3600 characters of concatenated text. An AI generator should respond by checking field queryability, tightening filters, fixing grouping, and truncating/aggregating LISTAGG inputs.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT LISTAGG(memo, ', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY id) FROM transactionline GROUP BY transaction -- long memos blow the ~3600-char limit and throw 'Invalid or unsupported search'
gotchas.example_right: SELECT LISTAGG(SUBSTR(memo,1,50), ', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY id) ... -- bound the concatenation; also verify fields in the Records Catalog and group all non-aggregates

gotchas.topic: Error 'Unknown identifier' — REST-catalog fields that are not SuiteQL-queryable
gotchas.rule: Not every field shown in the REST metadata catalog or Records Catalog is queryable in SuiteQL. Computed/summary fields (customer.balance, transaction 'total' variants, consolBalance), Time-of-Day custom fields, and Document-type custom fields raise 'Unknown identifier <field>' (400 Bad Request) in SELECT or WHERE. Compute equivalents via aggregation instead, and validate identifiers against actually-queryable columns.
gotchas.example_wrong: SELECT id, balance FROM customer -- 'Unknown identifier balance'
gotchas.example_right: SELECT t.entity, SUM(t.foreigntotal) AS balance FROM transaction t WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc' GROUP BY t.entity

gotchas.topic: NULL semantics: != and NOT IN silently drop NULL rows
gotchas.rule: Standard Oracle NULL semantics apply: inequality comparisons never match NULL, so filters like email != 'x' exclude records with no email at all. Add OR field IS NULL when 'different or empty' is intended; use NVL()/COALESCE() in aggregates (debit/credit are NULL, not 0, on transactionaccountingline).
gotchas.example_wrong: WHERE email != 'test@example.com' -- customers with NULL email vanish from results; SUM(credit - debit) returns NULL rows
gotchas.example_right: WHERE (email != 'test@example.com' OR email IS NULL); SUM(COALESCE(credit,0) - COALESCE(debit,0))

gotchas.topic: BUILTIN functions are a SuiteQL-only dialect feature; use RAW context by default
gotchas.rule: BUILTIN.DF/CF/CONSOLIDATE/CURRENCY_CONVERT/PERIOD/RELATIVE_RANGES/MNFILTER/HIERARCHY are SuiteQL extensions beyond SQL-92 (documented in 'SuiteQL Supported Built-in Functions'); they are not part of plain Oracle SQL and behave inconsistently or are unavailable through SuiteAnalytics Connect ODBC drivers — for queries that must also run over Connect, replace BUILTIN.DF with explicit joins to the referenced table. Field values default to the RAW context (internal IDs), which is why BUILTIN.DF exists for display values.
gotchas.example_wrong: Assuming a query using BUILTIN.DF(salesrep) is portable to an ODBC/Connect pipeline (community reports it failing to return display values there)
gotchas.example_right: SELECT e.entityid AS salesrep_name FROM transaction t LEFT JOIN employee e ON e.id = t.employee -- join-based display name, portable everywhere

gotchas.topic: Multi-select fields cannot be compared with = — use BUILTIN.MNFILTER, and its arguments are strings
gotchas.rule: Multi-select fields require BUILTIN.MNFILTER(field, 'MN_INCLUDE'|'MN_EXCLUDE', ..., 'FALSE', values...) instead of = or IN. Passing an unquoted boolean instead of the string 'FALSE' raises 'Search error occurred: Unknown identifier'.
gotchas.example_wrong: WHERE custrecord_multiselect = '5'  -- or BUILTIN.MNFILTER(field, 'MN_INCLUDE', '', false, '5') with bare false: Unknown identifier error
gotchas.example_right: WHERE BUILTIN.MNFILTER(custrecord_multiselect, 'MN_INCLUDE', '', 'FALSE', '5') = 'T'

cases.question: What was our total invoice revenue in Q1 2026?
cases.expected_outcome: Single aggregate from transaction headers: must filter type='CustInvc' AND a date scope resolving to 2026-01-01..2026-03-31 (trandate range, or postingperiod join to Jan/Feb/Mar 2026 periods). SUM(foreigntotal) minimally acceptable; a consultant prefers SUM(foreigntotal*exchangerate) labelled AUD or an explicit currency-mix caveat. Must not include SalesOrd or CustPymt.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: table 'transaction' present; matches /type\s*=\s*'CustInvc'/i; contains a date/period filter bounding Jan 1 2026 and Mar 31 2026 in any format, OR periodname IN ('Jan 2026','Feb 2026','Mar 2026'); does NOT match /'SalesOrd'|'CustPymt'/. RESULT: exactly 1 row with 1 numeric aggregate (label columns allowed).
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Summing foreigntotal across AUD/USD/GBP/EUR transactions without exchangerate yields a meaningless mixed-currency number in any real multi-subsidiary account.

cases.question: monthly net revenue for the trailing 12 months, counting credit memos as negative
cases.expected_outcome: transaction table; type IN ('CustInvc','CustCred') with a CASE (or signed UNION) negating CustCred amounts; GROUP BY month of trandate; rolling filter trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(SYSDATE,-12) or explicit Jul 2025 onward; ordered by month.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'CustInvc'/ AND /'CustCred'/; a negation construct bound to CustCred (e.g. /CASE[\s\S]*CustCred[\s\S]*THEN\s*-/i or subtraction of a credits aggregate); a month-bucket expression on trandate in GROUP BY; a rolling-12-month filter (ADD_MONTHS/SYSDATE) or explicit >= Jul 2025 bound. RESULT: 12-13 rows, columns (month, net_amount).
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Credit memo totals are stored as positive numbers — adding instead of subtracting them overstates revenue. The nearest few-shot ('Monthly invoice revenue trend') anchors the model to CustInvc-only with no credit netting.

cases.question: how much did we invoice last month
cases.expected_outcome: type='CustInvc'; calendar month before today (Jun 2026): bounded first-through-last day via TRUNC(SYSDATE,'MM')/ADD_MONTHS arithmetic, explicit 2026-06-01..2026-06-30, or periodname='Jun 2026'; single SUM(foreigntotal).
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /type\s*=\s*'CustInvc'/i; month-bounded filter resolving to Jun 1-30 2026 (SYSDATE month arithmetic, explicit dates, or periodname='Jun 2026'); does NOT use a rolling /SYSDATE\s*-\s*30/ window. RESULT: 1 row, 1 numeric aggregate.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: 'Last month' generated as a SYSDATE-30 rolling window instead of the prior calendar month — the figure never ties to the Jun 2026 period close and drifts day by day.

cases.question: weekly invoiced totals from 1 May 2026 to 30 June 2026
cases.expected_outcome: type='CustInvc'; trandate >= 2026-05-01 AND trandate <= 2026-06-30; GROUP BY week bucket (TO_CHAR(trandate,'IYYY-IW') or TRUNC(trandate,'IW')); ordered by week key.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /type\s*=\s*'CustInvc'/i; both boundary dates (May 1 2026 and Jun 30 2026) present in any parseable format; a week-grouping expression (/IW|WW/i inside TO_CHAR or TRUNC); ORDER BY the week key. RESULT: 8-10 rows, columns (week, total).
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Bare string date literals: real SuiteQL needs TO_DATE with an explicit mask; an AU account's DD/MM/YYYY date preference silently misparses comparisons written MM/DD-style.

cases.question: give me an AR aging with 0-30, 31-60, 61-90 and 90+ buckets by customer
cases.expected_outcome: transaction (type='CustInvc' AND status='open') joined to customer; age computed as SYSDATE - trandate (catalog has no duedate); pivoted SUM(CASE...) per bucket per customer, or long-form (customer,bucket,amount) rows spanning all four buckets; a per-customer total desirable.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transaction+customer; matches /'CustInvc'/ AND /status\s*=\s*'open'/i; boundary literals 30, 60 AND 90 all appear in CASE/filter logic; age derived from SYSDATE/CURRENT_DATE minus trandate; does NOT match /duedate|daysoverdue|amountremaining|foreignamountunpaid/i (columns absent from catalog). RESULT: one row per customer with >=4 numeric bucket columns, or long-form rows covering 4 distinct buckets.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Real aging runs on duedate and foreignamountunpaid/amountremaining — none exist in this catalog, so hallucinating t.duedate throws 'field not found'; using full foreigntotal overstates partially paid invoices.

cases.question: which customers currently owe us more than $50,000?
cases.expected_outcome: Open CustInvc grouped by customer with HAVING SUM(foreigntotal) > 50000, sorted descending; a stated assumption on whether unapplied credit memos net against the balance is acceptable either way.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'CustInvc'/ AND /status\s*=\s*'open'/i; joins customer; GROUP BY a customer identifier; HAVING (or outer filter) comparing an aggregate to 50000. RESULT: columns (customer, balance); every balance > 50000.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: status='open' is demo-only — live SuiteQL statuses are composite codes like 'CustInvc:A', so the literal 'open' returns zero rows on a real account; ignoring unapplied credits also overstates what customers owe.

cases.question: outstanding AR by subsidiary converted to AUD
cases.expected_outcome: Open CustInvc joined to subsidiary; SUM(foreigntotal * exchangerate) as the AUD figure; GROUP BY subsidiary name; optional local-currency subtotal alongside.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transaction+subsidiary; matches /'CustInvc'/ AND /status\s*=\s*'open'/i; contains /foreigntotal\s*\*\s*(t\.)?exchangerate/i (or reverse order); GROUP BY subsidiary name. RESULT: <=3 rows (AU/US/GB subsidiaries), columns (subsidiary, aud_amount).
cases.real_netsuite_trap: transaction.exchangerate is the historical booking rate to the subsidiary base; a real consolidated AR balance uses period-end consolidated rates, so booking-rate sums never tie to the consolidation report.

cases.question: show invoices that have been open for more than 60 days with the customer's email, oldest first
cases.expected_outcome: Detail rows: type='CustInvc' AND status='open' AND trandate < SYSDATE-60 (or SYSDATE-trandate > 60); join customer selecting email; columns tranid/trandate/amount/email; ORDER BY trandate ASC.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'CustInvc'/ AND /status\s*=\s*'open'/i; a 60-day age condition against trandate; joins customer; selects email; ORDER BY trandate ascending (or default); does NOT match /duedate/i. RESULT: detail rows, all trandates > 60 days before today, ascending.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Generators trained on real NetSuite reach for duedate or a daysopen virtual column — neither exists here; on live accounts aging keys off duedate, so trandate-based output won't match the AR aging report either.

cases.question: what's the balance of account 4000 for the Jul 2025 period?
cases.expected_outcome: transactionaccountingline JOIN account (acctnumber='4000') JOIN transaction JOIN accountingperiod ON t.postingperiod=ap.id AND ap.periodname='Jul 2025'; balance as SUM(credit)-SUM(debit) for an income account so revenue reads positive; single row.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transactionaccountingline+account+transaction+accountingperiod all present; matches /acctnumber\s*=\s*'4000'/ AND /periodname\s*=\s*'Jul 2025'/; join uses /postingperiod/i; does NOT substitute a trandate BETWEEN range for the period; expression references both credit and debit columns. RESULT: 1 row, 1 signed numeric; positive under credit-minus-debit convention.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Filtering trandate to July instead of postingperiod misses backdated/late postings; SUM(debit-credit) sign-flips a revenue account negative — a classic pasted-into-the-board-deck error.

cases.question: total expenses by GL account for Jan through Mar 2026
cases.expected_outcome: transactionaccountingline + account with accttype='Expense' (COGS inclusion acceptable if stated) + transaction; period scope Jan-Mar 2026 (postingperiod join preferred, trandate range acceptable if stated); SUM(debit)-SUM(credit) per account; GROUP BY acctnumber/fullname; descending order.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transactionaccountingline+account+transaction; accttype filter including /'Expense'/; date/period filter covering 2026-01-01..2026-03-31 or periodnames Jan/Feb/Mar 2026; GROUP BY account number or fullname; debit-minus-credit arithmetic. RESULT: one row per expense account; totals >= 0 under normal balances.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Without a posting filter a real account pulls non-posting documents into GL sums; answering from VendBill header totals instead of GL lines misclassifies bills coded to assets or COGS.

cases.question: net income for June 2026
cases.expected_outcome: GL-based: transactionaccountingline+account+transaction (+accountingperiod ideally); SUM(CASE Income: credit-debit) minus SUM(CASE COGS/Expense: debit-credit) scoped to Jun 2026 (periodname='Jun 2026' or trandate range); single row. Header foreigntotal is not an acceptable P&L source.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transactionaccountingline+account+transaction present; matches /'Income'/ AND (/'Expense'/ OR /'COGS'/) in accttype logic; a Jun 2026 filter (periodname or date range); arithmetic combines income and expense sides with opposite signs. RESULT: 1 row, 1 signed numeric.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Computing 'income' from invoice headers ignores COGS recognition, journals and non-invoice postings; including Bank/AcctRec/AcctPay accttypes turns the figure into balance-sheet noise.

cases.question: how much have we spent with Logistics vendors this year?
cases.expected_outcome: type='VendBill'; JOIN vendor ON v.id=t.entity with v.category='Logistics'; trandate >= 2026-01-01 (calendar-year assumption stated); SUM(foreigntotal) as scalar or per-vendor breakdown. Must join vendor, not customer.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /type\s*=\s*'VendBill'/i; joins vendor (NOT customer) on entity; matches /category\s*=\s*'Logistics'/; 2026 YTD filter; does NOT mix /'PurchOrd'|'VendPymt'/ into the same sum. RESULT: 1 aggregate row or one row per logistics vendor.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: transaction.entity serves both customer and vendor ids — joining customer.id compiles fine and silently returns empty/garbage; counting POs (unposted commitments) or payments alongside bills double-counts spend.

cases.question: monthly AP spend by vendor category over the last 6 months
cases.expected_outcome: type='VendBill'; join vendor; GROUP BY month(trandate) and v.category; filter trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(SYSDATE,-6) (roughly Jan-Jul 2026); ordered by month then category.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'VendBill'/; joins vendor; GROUP BY includes both a month expression and vendor category; rolling 6-month filter (ADD_MONTHS/SYSDATE) or explicit >= Jan 2026 bound. RESULT: columns (month, category, spend); category values drawn from Inventory/Logistics/Office/Services/Utilities.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Category lives on the vendor record, not the transaction — models invent t.category; including PurchOrd inflates 'spend' with commitments that never posted to AP.

cases.question: which vendors did we actually pay more than $20k in Q2 2026?
cases.expected_outcome: 'Actually pay' = cash out: type='VendPymt', join vendor, trandate 2026-04-01..2026-06-30, GROUP BY vendor HAVING SUM(foreigntotal) > 20000. A VendBill-based reading is acceptable only with an explicitly stated assumption that bills approximate payments.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'VendPymt'/ (OR matches /'VendBill'/ AND the response/SQL comment contains an explicit assumption statement); joins vendor; date bounds Apr 1 2026 and Jun 30 2026; aggregate compared to 20000. RESULT: (vendor, paid_total) rows all > 20000.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: On live accounts payment transactions carry negative or zero header totals and settle bills via applied links — HAVING SUM(total) > 20000 on raw payment headers returns nothing; bills-vs-payments is exactly the distinction naive generators blur.

cases.question: top 5 items by 2026 invoice revenue, and include ties for 5th place
cases.expected_outcome: transactionline+item+transaction; type='CustInvc'; 2026 filter; SUM(netamount) per item; tie handling via RANK()/DENSE_RANK() <= 5 in an outer filter, or FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS WITH TIES; ordered by revenue desc.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transactionline+item+transaction; matches /'CustInvc'/; 2026 filter; matches (/WITH\s+TIES/i OR /\b(DENSE_)?RANK\s*\(/i); does NOT rely on a bare /FETCH\s+FIRST\s+5\s+ROWS\s+ONLY/i without rank logic; does NOT match /\bLIMIT\b/i. RESULT: >=5 rows; every returned revenue >= the 5th-ranked value.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY silently drops tied items; LIMIT/TOP are not SuiteQL. On a real account invoice income lines carry NEGATIVE netamount, so ORDER BY SUM(netamount) DESC surfaces the worst sellers unless the sign is flipped.

cases.question: show me the 3 largest invoices we've ever issued
cases.expected_outcome: transaction type='CustInvc'; ORDER BY foreigntotal DESC; FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY; detail columns tranid/trandate/amount; customer name join desirable.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'CustInvc'/; ORDER BY the total descending; matches /FETCH\s+FIRST\s+3\s+ROWS?\s+ONLY/i; does NOT match /\bLIMIT\b/i or /\bTOP\s+3\b/i. RESULT: exactly 3 rows sorted descending by amount.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: LIMIT 3 (MySQL/Postgres) and TOP 3 (T-SQL) both throw syntax errors in SuiteQL — Oracle-style FETCH FIRST is mandatory.

cases.question: bottom 10 customers by 2025 invoice revenue — only customers who actually got invoiced
cases.expected_outcome: INNER JOIN customer to transaction; type='CustInvc'; 2025 range; GROUP BY customer; ORDER BY revenue ASC; FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY. The 'actually got invoiced' clause makes INNER (not LEFT) join correct.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /'CustInvc'/; 2025 filter; GROUP BY customer; ORDER BY the aggregate ascending; matches /FETCH\s+FIRST\s+10/i; customer join is INNER (no LEFT JOIN preserving un-invoiced customers). RESULT: 10 rows, all revenue > 0, ascending order.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: A reflexive LEFT JOIN drags never-invoiced customers in as NULL/zero rows, contradicting the request; ties at the 10th slot get cut arbitrarily without a deterministic tiebreaker.

cases.question: revenue posted to the Mar 2026 accounting period, even where the transaction date fell in a different month
cases.expected_outcome: MUST resolve the period via t.postingperiod -> accountingperiod with periodname='Mar 2026'; revenue filter type='CustInvc' (or GL Income variant); trandate must NOT appear as a range filter doing the period's job; scalar or period-keyed rows.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: table accountingperiod present; /postingperiod/i used in a join or IN-subquery; matches /periodname\s*=\s*'Mar 2026'/; revenue filter (/'CustInvc'/ or Income accttype logic); trandate does NOT appear in a WHERE range predicate (SELECT-list display allowed). RESULT: 1 aggregate row (or rows keyed only by the period).
cases.real_netsuite_trap: trandate BETWEEN 1 Mar and 31 Mar is the classic wrong answer — backdated and late-posted entries diverge from it; and the period name must match NetSuite's exact format ('Mar 2026', not 'March 2026').

cases.question: which accounting periods are still open, and how much invoice revenue is posted into each?
cases.expected_outcome: accountingperiod WHERE closed='F'; LEFT JOIN transaction ON postingperiod match with type='CustInvc' inside the ON clause (or pre-filtered subquery); GROUP BY periodname; open periods with zero postings still listed; ordered by startdate.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: matches /closed\s*=\s*'F'/; LEFT (OUTER) JOIN to transaction or a correlated subquery keyed on postingperiod; the CustInvc condition NOT placed in a bare outer WHERE on the transaction side; GROUP BY period. RESULT: exactly 4 rows (Apr 2026, May 2026, Jun 2026, Jul 2026); amounts >= 0 with NULL/COALESCE tolerated for empty periods.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: closed is a 'T'/'F' string, not a boolean (closed = false errors out); INNER JOIN or WHERE t.type='CustInvc' after the LEFT JOIN drops the zero-activity future periods the controller asked about.

cases.question: for Q1 2026 compare invoice totals by transaction-date month vs by posting period — I want to spot backdated postings
cases.expected_outcome: Two aggregations over CustInvc Jan-Mar 2026: one keyed on TO_CHAR(trandate,'YYYY-MM'), one keyed on accountingperiod.periodname via postingperiod; presented side-by-side (FULL/LEFT join of two subqueries or a labeled UNION), ideally with a difference column.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: contains BOTH a trandate-derived month expression AND a postingperiod->accountingperiod join; matches /'CustInvc'/; Q1 2026 bounds applied to each side. RESULT: per month/period two comparable totals (two numeric columns, or labeled rows) covering Jan-Mar 2026 such that a difference is computable.
cases.real_netsuite_trap: Treating trandate month and posting period as interchangeable is precisely the bug being probed; aligning the keys also trips models — '2026-01' and 'Jan 2026' never join as raw strings without an explicit mapping.

cases.question: break down 2026 invoice revenue by product line
cases.expected_outcome: transactionline JOIN classification ON l.class JOIN transaction (type='CustInvc', 2026); SUM(netamount) GROUP BY classification.name yielding Hardware/Software/Services. Must source class from the line, not the header and not item.class.
cases.validation_predicate: SQL: tables transactionline+classification+transaction; classification joined from transactionline.class (NOT transaction.class which doesn't exist, NOT item.class); matches /'CustInvc'/; 2026 filter; GROUP BY classification name. RESULT: <=3 rows named among Hardware/Software/Services.
